OBJECTION HEARING CALENDAR
Off Calendar Apr-15-2015 Off Calendar

City And County Of San Franciscos Demurrer To The Complaint Of Sj Amoroso Construction Company, Inc.
Off Calendar Feb-06-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. A. ROBERT ROSIN of Leonidou & Rosin, PC, Tel No. (650) 691-2888 appearing for Plaintiff S.J. Amoroso Construction Co., Inc.;
Deputy City Attorney JOSEPH SANDOVAL, JR., Tel No. (415) 554-3867 appearing for Defendant City and County of San Francisco.

RULING - A R G U E D - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. As to DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Demurrer To The Complaint Of S.J. Amoroso Construction Company, Inc., the demurrer to the second cause of action for equitable indemnity and the third cause of action for contribution is sustained without leave to amend. The complaint alleges breach of contractual duties and does not state a cause of action for negligence against Defendant. Further, Plaintiff admits in its opposition that "[e]ach of the obligations that the City has breached are founded upon the written contract for the Project." (Plaintiff's opposition brief, page 13.) Implied contractual indemnity is not available when the parties have a contract that contains an express indemnity clause. For example, in Maryland Casualty Co. v. Bailey and Sons, Inc. (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 856, 873, the court held: "Where parties expressly contract with respect to the duty to indemnify, the extent of that duty must be determined from the contract and not by reliance on the independent doctrine of equitable indemnity. This rule seeks to permit people to voluntarily order their affairs in a manner agreeable to them and recognizes that equity rarely interferes with a contract knowledgeably executed." Other cases have reached the same conclusion. (See, e.g. Prince v. PG&E (2009) 45 Cal.4th 1151, 1158, "Express indemnity generally is not subject to equitable considerations or a joint legal obligation to the injured party; rather, it is enforced in accordance with the terms of the contracting parties' agreement; Markley v. Beagle (1967) 66 Cal.2d 951, 961, "Since the parties expressly contracted with respect to the contractors' duty to indemnify the owners, the extent of that duty must be determined from the contract and not from the independent doctrine of equitable indemnity.") Here, the contract entered into by Plaintiff and Defendant, attached to the complaint, contains an express indemnity provision at section 74. Thus, the equitable indemnity cause of action fails as a matter of law. The cases cited by Plaintiff in opposition, including E.L. White, Inc. v. Huntington Beach (1978) 21 Cal.3d 497, apply equitable indemnity principles when there is a tort cause of action, and therefore, do not apply to the case at hand. The complaint also does not state a cause of action for contribution, as there are no allegations that Defendant is a joint tortfeasor. (See Western Steamship Lines, Inc. v. San Pedro Peninsula Hosp. (1994) 8 Cal.4th 100, 108, fn. 6.) PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL REQUESTS TIME TO REVIEW DEFENDANT'S PROPOSED ORDER. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)
